attributes.stem:
A 19-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a six-hour history of fever, vomiting, and diarrhoea. She states that she notices a malodor from her vagina for 1 week. Her last menses was 2 weeks ago and she uses a combination of super pads and tampons for menorrhagia. She has a regular partner and her last sexually transmitted infection tests were negative. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications except for the combined oral contraceptive pill.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 39.2°C. She has a diffuse, blanching, erythematous rash resembling sunburn over her trunk and extremities. Her mucous membranes are hyperaemic.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of features suggesting toxic shock syndrome (TSS): high fever, diffuse blanching erythematous rash (like sunburn), and hypotension, likely secondary to a retained tampon from the last menses. The temporal association with menstruation and tampon use, along with multi-system involvement (gastrointestinal symptoms, mucosal hyperaemia) strongly supports this diagnosis. Desquamation of the skin including the soles and palms usually occurs 1 - 2 weeks after the onset of illness.

TSS is caused by toxin-producing Staphylococcus aureus or streptococcus pyogenes. Staphylococcus aureus infections caused by retained tampons were more common in the 1980s with the invention of super absorbent tampons, but this is rarer now with increased public knowledge (though this can still occur with diaphragms and menstrual cups). This is called menstrual toxic shock-like syndrome. Non-menstrual toxic shock-like syndrome is now typically due to soft tissue infections.

Meningococcal septicaemia typically presents with a non-blanching purpuric rash rather than the blanching erythematous rash seen here. While it can cause shock, the association with menstruation and the nature of the rash make this diagnosis less likely.

Secondary syphilis can present with a generalised rash and constitutional symptoms, but typically follows a painless chancre by 4-10 weeks. The rash is typically copper-coloured, and shock would be unusual. The acute onset and severity of illness make this diagnosis unlikely.

Systemic lupus erythematosus can present with fever and rash but typically has a more insidious onset with joint pain, fatigue, and other systemic symptoms developing over weeks to months. The acute presentation and shock state would be unusual.

Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome typically affects children under five years of age and presents with tender erythema that develops into large, flaccid blisters and desquamation. While it is also caused by Staphylococcus aureus, it lacks the multi-system involvement seen in TSS.

The diagnosis of TSS is clinical and requires a high index of suspicion. Early recognition is crucial as the condition can rapidly progress to multi-organ failure if not promptly treated with tampon removal, fluid resuscitation, and appropriate antibiotics.
learningPoints: The clinical triad of fever, rash, and hypotension during menstruation is characteristic of toxic shock syndrome.
Multi-organ involvement including gastrointestinal, mucosal, and cardiovascular systems is typical in toxic shock syndrome.
references: Silva JM, Gomes Cochicho J, Carvalho E, Parente AR, Cruz Nodarse A, Pádua F. Streptococcal Toxic Shock Syndrome: A Case Report. Cureus. 2022. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9839978/

Atchade E, De Tymowski C, Grall N, Tanaka S, Montravers P. Toxic Shock Syndrome: A Literature Review. Antibiotics: Infection Diagnostics and Antimicrobial Therapy for Critical Patient. 2024. Available at: https://www.mdpi.com/2079-6382/13/1/96
